agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a reduction in the precision of delivery promises communicated to end users and potentially integrated partners. In logistics, ETA precision is a core competitive differentiator and directly tied to customer satisfaction, SLA enforcement, and contractual obligations.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / ETA Presentation","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the predicted date/time a shipment will reach its destination, communicated to the recipient."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"A time range within which delivery is promised. Can be an exact timestamp, a time range within a day, or a full-day range."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"A contractual commitment defining delivery performance thresholds, often tied to ETA precision and on-time delivery rates."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery","definition":"A delivery that arrives within the promised ETA window. Widening the window mechanically increases on-time rates without improving actual performance."},{"term":"Last-Mile Delivery","definition":"The final leg of the delivery chain from distribution center to recipient — where ETA precision matters most to the end customer."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery ETAs must reflect a commitment the logistics operation can realistically fulfill — the precision of the ETA must be calibrated to the precision of the underlying prediction model.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Promising precision that cannot be delivered erodes trust and generates support costs. Conversely, artificially reducing precision hides operational performance from customers.","testable_assertion":"The ETA display precision should not exceed the confidence interval of the prediction model, and should not be reduced below what the model can reliably predict."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Any change to ETA precision must preserve the ability to measure and enforce SLAs against the same standard before and after the change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If on-time delivery is measured against 'by Friday' instead of 'by 14:30 Friday,' the metric becomes meaningless — a delivery at 23:59 Friday counts the same as 08:00. This corrupts operational accountability.","testable_assertion":"SLA measurement queries must use the same precision as the ETA commitment; changing display precision must not silently change SLA evaluation logic."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"ETA display changes must not break downstream integrations that consume ETA data (partner APIs, webhook payloads, tracking page embeds).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Partners and integrated systems may depend on timestamp-precision ETAs for their own scheduling, staffing, and customer communication. Removing time-of-day from the API is a breaking change.","testable_assertion":"API contracts that currently return datetime ETAs must continue to do so, or be versioned with a deprecation period."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Customers who paid for premium/express delivery tiers may have contractual rights to time-specific ETAs.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy tied to service tier definitions, not a system integrity constraint. It can be changed by product decision, but doing so has financial and trust implications.","testable_assertion":"If premium delivery tiers exist, verify that date-only ETAs do not violate tier-specific SLA language."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"The current system displays ETAs as exact timestamps","when":"The change to date-only windows is implemented","then":"All downstream API consumers must be identified and either (a) continue receiving timestamp-precision ETAs or (b) be notified and given a migration window."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"SLAs are currently measured against timestamp-precision ETAs","when":"Display precision is reduced to date-only","then":"SLA measurement logic must be explicitly reviewed and a decision documented on whether SLA thresholds change or remain timestamp-based internally."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A customer views a delivery ETA as 'Delivery by Friday'","when":"The delivery arrives at 23:58 on Friday","then":"The system must count this as on-time, which means the business has accepted that end-of-day Friday is the commitment."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Multiple delivery service tiers exist (standard, express, same-day)","when":"Date-only ETAs are applied","then":"Express and same-day tiers must be excluded from this change OR their SLA definitions must be explicitly renegotiated, since 'same-day' with no time is meaningless."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A partner integration currently consumes timestamp ETAs via API","when":"The ETA response format changes to date-only","then":"The API change must be treated as a breaking change with proper versioning and deprecation notice."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Same-day delivery displayed as date-only","expected_behavior":"'Delivery by today' with no time is functionally useless to the customer. Same-day and express tiers must retain time-of-day precision or the product promise is broken."},{"scenario":"Delivery spanning midnight (e.g., late-night logistics)","expected_behavior":"A shipment predicted for 01:00 Saturday vs 23:00 Friday — date-only display puts these in different days. The system must handle timezone-aware day boundaries correctly."},{"scenario":"Multi-leg international shipments crossing timezones","expected_behavior":"'Delivery by Friday' in which timezone? Origin? Destination? UTC? Must be explicitly defined and displayed."},{"scenario":"Customer plans to be home based on ETA","expected_behavior":"Date-only windows force customers to be available all day. For high-value or signature-required deliveries, this significantly degrades customer experience."},{"scenario":"SLA reporting before and after the change","expected_behavior":"Historical on-time metrics become incomparable. A delivery that was 'late' under timestamp SLA may be 'on-time' under date-only SLA. Reporting must clearly demarcate the regime change."},{"scenario":"B2B customers with receiving dock schedules","expected_behavior":"Business recipients often have specific receiving hours. Date-only ETAs provide no actionable scheduling information, potentially causing failed deliveries and redelivery costs."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reducing ETA precision may artificially inflate on-time delivery metrics, masking operational inefficiencies that cost money. If premium delivery tiers lose their precision advantage, customers may downgrade — directly reducing revenue per shipment. Failed deliveries due to customer unavailability (they couldn't plan around a vague ETA) increase redelivery costs, typically $5-15 per attempt. Conversely, if the current system frequently misses exact timestamps, the brand damage from 'late' deliveries may exceed the cost of reduced precision.","operational":"Operationally, wider windows reduce pressure on dispatchers and route optimizers, which could improve driver utilization and reduce overtime. However, this masks root-cause issues in prediction accuracy. SLA measurement and reporting must be rebuilt or recalibrated, which is non-trivial. Internal dashboards, partner reporting, and contractual compliance checks all need review.","user":"This is a significant UX regression for customers who rely on time-specific ETAs to plan their day — particularly for residential deliveries where someone must be present. Customer satisfaction surveys in logistics consistently show ETA precision as a top-3 driver of NPS. Moving from timestamp to date-only puts the service behind competitors who offer 1-2 hour windows. For B2B customers, this may be a dealbreaker.","risk":"High risk of silent SLA redefinition — metrics improve overnight without operational improvement, creating a false sense of progress. Risk of contract violations if existing agreements specify timestamp-level ETAs. Risk of partner integration breakage if API contracts change. Competitive risk as industry trend is toward narrower windows, not wider ones.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (B2C)","impact":"Lose ability to plan their day around delivery; must remain available all day. Signature-required deliveries become especially painful. Likely increase in missed deliveries and support calls."},{"group":"Business Customers (B2B)","impact":"Cannot schedule receiving dock staff or coordinate warehouse intake. May violate their own internal SLAs. Highest risk of churn from this change."},{"group":"Partner Integrations / API Consumers","impact":"Breaking change if API payload loses timestamp precision. Requires migration path and communication."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch Teams","impact":"Reduced pressure to hit exact windows — mixed blessing. May improve morale but masks prediction model deficiencies."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Likely increase in 'where is my package' contacts when customers can't narrow down arrival time. Support volume may increase 10-20%."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"Harder to sell premium tiers if the ETA promise is less precise. B2B accounts may push back during renewal."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Risk of premium tier downgrades. Potential savings from fewer 'late' penalty payouts if SLAs are loosened, but this is an accounting trick, not operational improvement."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal lacks critical business context. No rationale was given for why the engineering team wants this change (prediction model limitations? UI simplification? reducing false precision?). Without knowing the motivation, current SLA structure, customer segment breakdown, and contractual obligations, the analysis is necessarily speculative. The domain implications are clear, but whether this change is net-positive or net-negative depends entirely on context that was never provided.","high_confidence_areas":["ETA precision is a core domain concept in logistics with well-understood customer impact","Reducing display precision has direct SLA measurement implications","API contract changes require versioning","Same-day and express tiers are incompatible with date-only ETAs"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the current prediction model actually supports timestamp-level accuracy","Current SLA contractual language and whether it mandates time precision","Customer segment mix (B2B vs B2C ratio) which determines severity","Whether this is display-only or also affects internal ETA storage","The business rationale driving this proposal"],"assumptions":["The system currently has both B2C and B2B customers","There are multiple delivery service tiers including express/same-day","ETAs are exposed via API to partner integrations, not just UI","SLA measurement currently uses timestamp precision"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No business rationale provided for why the engineering team wants to reduce ETA precision. Is the prediction model unable to support timestamp accuracy? Is this a UI simplification effort? Are exact ETAs causing more harm than good due to frequent misses? The correct domain analysis depends entirely on the motivation.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Engineering","All customer segments"],"options":["Request the business rationale from the engineering team before proceeding","Request current on-time delivery rates at timestamp precision to understand if the model supports it"],"recommendation":"Do not proceed until the business rationale is documented. If the prediction model cannot support timestamp precision, the correct solution may be time-range windows (e.g., '2-4 PM Friday') rather than date-only."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Current SLA structure and contractual obligations around ETA precision are unknown. If contracts specify timestamp-level ETAs, this change may constitute a breach.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Account Management","B2B Customers"],"options":["Obtain current SLA templates and key account contracts","Have Legal review before any change to ETA precision"],"recommendation":"Legal and Account Management must review existing contracts before this change is approved."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Customer segment breakdown (B2B vs B2C) and delivery tier distribution are unknown. The impact severity depends heavily on what percentage of deliveries are premium/express and what percentage of customers are businesses with receiving schedules.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Sales"],"options":["Pull delivery tier distribution data","Survey key B2B accounts on ETA precision requirements"],"recommendation":"Quantify the customer base affected before making a blanket change. Consider applying date-only windows only to standard-tier B2C deliveries as a scoped pilot."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"It is unknown whether this change affects only the UI display layer or also the API response payloads and internal ETA storage. A display-only change has far less blast radius than an end-to-end precision reduction.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Partner Integrations","Operations"],"options":["Clarify scope: display-only vs API vs internal storage","If display-only, keep timestamp precision in APIs and internal systems"],"recommendation":"At minimum, internal systems and APIs should retain timestamp precision regardless of what is shown to end users."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"No business rationale documented for reducing a core competitive differentiator","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 — ETA precision must be calibrated to prediction model capability, not arbitrarily reduced"},{"violation":"Impact on existing SLA contracts has not been assessed","business_rule_broken":"BR-2 — SLA measurement standard must be preserved or explicitly renegotiated"},{"violation":"Same-day and express delivery tiers become meaningless with date-only ETAs","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — Premium tier SLA language must not be violated"},{"violation":"API breaking change implications not addressed","business_rule_broken":"BR-3 — Downstream integrations must not be silently broken"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA display from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a reduction in the precision of delivery promises communicated to end users and potentially integrated partners. In logistics, ETA precision is a core competitive differentiator and directly tied to customer satisfaction, SLA enforcement, and contractual obligations.","bounded_con

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this ETA display change from a domain perspective.

This is a change from exact timestamp ETAs to date-only delivery windows. Let me think through the business implications carefully.

This is an ambiguity case - the proposal is vague. "Date-only windows" could mean several things, and the business rationale isn't stated. But actually, the key issue is missing context - we don't know WHY they want this change, what SLAs exist, what contractual obligations exist around ETA precision, etc.

Wait - the scenario says "the engineering team wants to change" but doesn't say why. The business rationale is never stated by anyone. 

I need to identify what's actually clear versus what's missing before I can assess the impact. The current system shows exact timestamps like "Delivery by 14:30 on Friday," while the proposal moves to date-only windows like "Delivery by Friday" - which is fundamentally a loss of precision in our delivery commitments. But without understanding the underlying motivation, current SLA commitments, or what our contracts actually require, I can't properly evaluate whether this is a reasonable trade-off or a risky move.
